summary: Rifle's affordable-housing pipeline runs through the local Rifle Housing Authority (RHA, founded 1979, senior / disability focused) and an active set of LIHTC projects: Rifle Apartments (60 units, CHFA 2023 9% LIHTC, opened April 2026) — the first family LIHTC built in Rifle in roughly 20 years — and Maxfield Heights Senior Apartments (50 units, TWG + RHA, completed 2021). The city's 2025 'Rooted in Rifle' Housing Needs Assessment guides ongoing policy.

paragraphs.text: Per CHFA's 2023 Round One award narrative, no family-property LIHTC project had been built in Rifle since 2002. Rifle Apartments (60 units; 30 1-BR, 20 2-BR, 10 3-BR at 30%-80% AMI, with 14 project-based vouchers at or below 30% AMI from the county housing authority) is the city's first family LIHTC in roughly 20 years.

CHFA awarded Rifle Apartments a 9% LIHTC reservation in 2023 Round One — $1,450,000 in annual federal credits supporting a total development cost of $28,403,918 (media-reported final cost $29.2M with approximately $12.3M net LIHTC equity). Developer Evergreen Real Estate Group, GC Shaw Construction. Site is 4.7 acres along Rifle Creek in the Creekside Neighborhood Sub-District of the downtown commercial core; non-metro DDA.
Groundbreaking was September 27, 2024. The grand opening was held April 17, 2026, with 95% lease-up at opening.

Maxfield Heights Senior Apartments (125 Ute Avenue) is a 50-unit, 1-BR / 1-BA LIHTC senior building (55+) at 30%-60% AMI, with 8 project-based vouchers at or below 30% AMI. Developer TWG Development partnered with RHA; construction began Spring 2020 and the building opened in 2021.

Two older Rifle LIHTC properties remain in service: Eagles Nest Apartments (1041 W 24th Street, 30 LIHTC family units, placed in service in 2000) — the CHFA Rifle Apartments narrative reported zero vacancy and a waitlist of 43 families — and White River Village (800 S Whiteriver Avenue, 29 LIHTC family townhomes built in 2005) with zero vacancy and a waitlist of over 100 families.

RHA was established in 1979 to provide affordable rental housing for senior and disabled residents. The agency operates approximately 106 low-income apartments — 60 HUD rental-assistance / Section 8 units and 46 affordable units — with roughly 150 households on the waitlist per recent reporting.
The City of Rifle opted in to Colorado Proposition 123 ahead of the November 1, 2023 commitment deadline, committing to grow its affordable-housing stock by 3% per year over a 3-year cycle.

Rifle's documented direct financial commitment to the Rifle Apartments project was $600,000 (CHFA narrative §2d). The city also signed a Letter of Support endorsing the project, and supported the developer's HB 22-1304 Infrastructure & Strong Communities funding request to DOLA ($5,000,000) and DOH Gap Funds request ($3,000,000).

City Council unanimously approved leasing a 10-acre site at 2515 Centennial Parkway (a former uranium-production site adjacent to wastewater treatment) to Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley for $10 over 50 years. The 63,000 sq ft modular production and advanced manufacturing training facility plans production of 100-200 modular homes per year, partnering with Colorado Mountain College and Colorado River BOCES for workforce training. Construction began in early 2025.

In November 2025 Rifle voters rejected a lodging-tax ballot measure that could have funded affordable housing under Colorado's updated framework (which allows up to 6% with voter approval); Rifle continues to rely on Prop 123, state DOH gap funds, and ad-hoc council line-item allocations rather than a dedicated housing trust fund.

The 'Rooted in Rifle' Housing Needs Assessment (2025, Points Consulting) found that Rifle has roughly 3,600 housing units (many over 20 years old), average home cost around $490,000 (a 133% increase relative to baseline), median household income up only 55%, and 17% of renters cost-burdened.

Wapiti Commons (Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley) is a 20-home neighborhood south of I-70 in Rifle. As of August 2025, 14 homeowners had moved in; the final 6 closed in early fall 2025. Three-bedroom townhomes started at $295,000 in the program. The county BoCC purchased 2 of the units for possible county-worker housing.

The EcoDwelling subdivision — approved by Council on November 16 — programs 17 single-family units (each approximately 900 sq ft, 2-BR / 2-BA) on a 1.93-acre site at West 14th Street Marketplace. Price range $265,000-$270,000 per unit, roughly half Rifle's average single-family home price. Phase 1 includes the 17 lots; the larger pipeline approaches 200 units.

Per Aspen Daily News reporting, cities along Highway 82 (Aspen, Snowmass Village, Basalt, Carbondale, and Glenwood Springs) signed up for the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC); the I-70 / Colorado River Valley jurisdictions — Rifle among them — held off, at least at the time of reporting. WMRHC continues to coordinate with non-member jurisdictions including Rifle.
